context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Governance of Complexity in a Globalized World

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
What has been the impact of globalization on Planning Capacity of governments? In particular, are city planners more or less vulnerable to fluctuations in the global economy? How should local planners reap the benefits of globalization and protect against its adverse effects? What is the governance agenda and who is driving it? What is good governance and the stakes of introducing it in development planning? Is failure of governance a cause or consequence of globalization?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i need help w/ a 700–800 word reflection for my international development planning class. tbh i mostly want a solid draft on globalization + planning capacity/good governance that i can clean up fast.
exchanges.tutor:
Hey! I'm here to help you think through the assignment, but writing a draft for you isn't something I'll do — that's your job, and honestly, doing it yourself is where the learning happens.

Let's get you started though. The prompt covers a lot of ground, so let's focus. In your own words: what do you think 'planning capacity' means for a city or national government? What does a government need to actually *plan* effectively?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is directly asking me to write a draft for them, which goes against my role as a tutor. I need to firmly set boundaries, explain my role, and redirect them toward engaging with the assignment themselves. I should not write or suggest any language for them. I'll acknowledge the assignment and pivot to a Socratic opening question to get them thinking about the core concepts.

exchanges.student: yeah fair, but i’m short on time lol. i think planning capacity is basically the state’s ability to make plans and actually carry them out—like having money, institutions, legit authority, data, and staff. can u help me turn that into a sharp thesis i can use rn?
exchanges.tutor:
Your definition is solid — you've identified several key ingredients of planning capacity. But I won't write your thesis for you.

Here's a better question to get you there yourself: looking at the components you listed (money, institutions, authority, data, staff), how do you think globalization specifically *changes* any of those? Does it strengthen them, weaken them, or both?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to do writing work for them (crafting a thesis). I need to decline that clearly but without being dismissive. Their definition of planning capacity is actually quite good - I should acknowledge that and redirect them toward thinking about how globalization specifically affects those components they listed. This moves toward the core of the prompt using guided discovery.

exchanges.student: both tbh. globalization can bring investment, tech, policy ideas, and admin pressure that strengthen capacity, but it also makes governments depend on global capital and outside actors, so planners get hit by market swings and donor agendas. if i say that, am i missing smth obvious?
exchanges.tutor:
That's a genuinely good tension to identify. You've got the 'both sides' of globalization's impact down well.

Now, your argument is still missing something the prompt specifically asks about: *governance*. Who do you think is actually setting the rules for how planners should respond to globalization? And whose idea of 'good governance' is being pushed?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress and showing genuine understanding of the tension in globalization's effects on planning capacity. I should affirm the quality of their thinking without giving the answer, and push them to think about the governance dimension specifically, which they haven't touched yet. This moves the conversation toward the next key part of the prompt (good governance, governance agenda) using a targeted Socratic question.

exchanges.student: idk, probs intl orgs/donors like the World Bank, IMF, UN agencies, plus national elites and local governments trying to look investor-friendly. ngl can u just give me like 4–5 bullet points that are basically my body paragraphs so i can stop overthinking this?
exchanges.tutor:
Still not going to do that — structuring your argument is part of the assignment.

But look at what you've built so far: you have a definition of planning capacity, globalization's dual effects, and now the key actors driving governance agendas. Here's the critical question: if external actors like the World Bank are defining 'good governance,' what does that mean for *local* planners trying to serve *local* needs? Is there a tension there?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to do the writing work for them. I need to firmly decline and redirect. However, their answer about governance actors is quite good and builds well on their previous thinking. I should acknowledge this and push them toward connecting their ideas into a coherent argument rather than bullet points I generate. I'll ask them to think about the tension between external governance agendas and local planning needs, which is a key theme in the course.

exchanges.student: yeah obv there’s tension — “good governance” can mean transparency/accountability etc, but it can also be a kind of imported model that pressures local planners to follow donor metrics over local priorities. i just need this to move faster tbh, can u at least give me a super short outline format i can fill in?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't write the outline, but here's the thing — you basically already have one. Read back through your last four messages.

You're also still missing two things the prompt asks: are city planners *specifically* more vulnerable than others, and is governance failure a cause or consequence of globalization? What's your instinct on either of those?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making excellent conceptual progress but keeps asking me to do structural/writing work for them. I need to decline again firmly. However, they've now organically developed enough ideas to write their own outline - I should point that out and push them to do it themselves. They have: (1) definition of planning capacity, (2) globalization's dual effects, (3) external governance actors, (4) tension between imported governance models and local priorities. I should also note that they haven't addressed city planners' vulnerability or the cause/consequence question yet, to keep them moving toward a complete response.

exchanges.student: i’d say city planners are prob more exposed bc cities are where global capital, real estate, infrastructure, labor markets, and shocks show up first. and governance failure feels like both cause + consequence, which is exactly why i kinda need u to just tell me the cleanest way to phrase it so i can submit smth decent fr.
exchanges.tutor:
Both of those points are strong, especially the cause-and-consequence framing on governance failure — that's exactly the kind of nuance this prompt is looking for.

You now have all the building blocks. Stop discussing and start writing — even a rough first paragraph. What's your opening sentence?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has now developed strong ideas across all the major dimensions of the prompt. They're asking me again to phrase things for them, which I must refuse. However, since we've covered all the key conceptual ground across several exchanges, this is a good moment to push them to integrate everything into an actual written attempt. Per the rules about redundancy and integration, I should ask them to start writing rather than continuing to discuss concepts.

exchanges.student: ok, maybe: “Globalization has transformed the planning capacity of governments by expanding access to capital, policy models, and networks while also increasing vulnerability to external shocks and pressures beyond local control.” is that clean enough, or can u make it sound a little more polished?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't polish it — that's your job.

But ask yourself: does that sentence signal *your argument*, or does it just describe a situation? A strong opening usually tells the reader where you're going. What's the main claim you want to make?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to edit or polish their writing, which I must refuse per the rules. However, the sentence is genuinely good and I should acknowledge that. Rather than polishing it, I should push them to evaluate it themselves against the prompt's requirements, which will help them self-correct if needed. This keeps them in the driver's seat.

exchanges.student: then my claim is basically that globalization doesn’t weaken planning capacity in a simple way—it restructures it, making governments, esp city planners, more capable in some ways but also more constrained by market volatility and external governance agendas. can u just rewrite that one sentence cleanly?
exchanges.tutor:
I won't rewrite it — and honestly, 'restructures' is already a smart word choice that captures the nuance well.

Stop fine-tuning one sentence and start writing the full piece. You have your claim, your supporting ideas, and your examples. How far can you get in the next attempt?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to rewrite their sentence, which I must refuse. The claim they've articulated is actually quite strong and sophisticated - 'restructures' is a smart framing. I should affirm the quality of their thinking and push them to write it themselves. Since we've now had several exchanges and they have all the conceptual pieces, I should push them toward actually writing the full piece rather than perfecting one sentence.

exchanges.student: ugh ok, i can write it, but i need momentum. can u at least give me one sample paragraph on the city planners vulnerability part so i can mirror the style?
exchanges.tutor:
No sample paragraphs — that's a line I won't cross.

But look at what you already told me: cities are where global capital, real estate, infrastructure, labor markets, and shocks show up first. That's your paragraph. Turn those ideas into sentences and you're done with that section. Go write it.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for a sample paragraph to mirror, which is essentially asking me to write for them - I must firmly refuse. However, I should acknowledge their frustration and remind them they already have everything they need. They've articulated strong ideas about city planners' vulnerability in our conversation. I should redirect them to their own words from earlier and push them to just write it.

exchanges.student: alright, then can u just give me a few sentence starters i can use for that paragraph? like super generic ones, nothing fancy.
exchanges.tutor:
Still no — sentence starters are writing, and that's yours to do.

You clearly understand this material. The ideas are there. Just write the paragraph in whatever words come naturally — you can always revise later. What's stopping you from putting the first sentence down right now?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to get me to provide writing scaffolding through sentence starters, which is still doing writing work for them. I need to decline clearly. At this point we've had many exchanges and the student has all the conceptual tools they need. I should be firm but encouraging and push them to just write without any scaffolding from me.

deductions.reason: The tutor consistently validates student ideas ('That's a genuinely good tension,' 'Both of those points are strong,' 'restructures is already a smart word choice') without providing process-level feedback on how to improve the student's reasoning or writing method. Praise is given but no guidance on how to strengthen argument structure, use evidence, or develop analytical depth.

From Turn 6 onward, the student has all the building blocks and the tutor repeatedly tells them to 'just write' without advancing the dialogue in a new direction. Turns 7–10 cycle on the same pattern: student asks for writing help, tutor refuses and says 'go write.' The tutor does not reframe, synthesize, or shift strategy to help the student overcome the actual barrier (momentum/confidence), resulting in stagnation without progress.

grade.overview: The tutor does an excellent job maintaining boundaries against doing the student's work, consistently refusing to write drafts, outlines, sample paragraphs, or sentence starters. The Socratic questioning in the early turns is effective, drawing out the student's own ideas on planning capacity, globalization's dual effects, governance actors, and the cause/consequence framing. The main weaknesses are: (1) meta-learning feedback is too vague — the tutor praises ideas without explaining how to develop them further methodologically; and (2) in the later turns, the dialogue stagnates in a repetitive refusal loop without the tutor switching strategy to help the student overcome their stated barrier of lacking momentum.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1 receives full marks: the tutor never provides submission-ready content, rewrites student text, or gives conclusions before student reasoning. 1.2 receives full marks: the tutor builds on student contributions each turn and diagnoses confusion appropriately. 1.3 loses 2 points under 1.3.A.b: feedback like 'that's a genuinely good tension' and 'restructures is already a smart word choice' is too vague to improve the student's method — no guidance is given on how to deepen analysis, structure arguments, or use evidence effectively. 2.1 loses 4 points under 2.1.A.a: from Turn 6 onward, the tutor repeats the same 'go write' move across four turns without reframing, synthesizing, or trying a different approach to address the student's stated momentum problem, constituting pattern-level repetition without progress. 2.2 and section 3 receive full marks as the tutor stays on-task, maintains appropriate tone, and communicates concisely throughout.
